profile.summary: A mid-level Backend Engineer builds and maintains server-side services, APIs, data flows, and integrations. They usually own delivery of well-scoped features, debugging, testing, and production fixes within a team. They work closely with product, frontend, platform, QA, and other backend engineers, and are expected to write reliable code, handle routine operational issues, and contribute to design discussions without owning broad technical direction.

known_challenges.text: Balancing feature delivery with fixing reliability, performance, and technical debt in existing services.
Debugging production issues across logs, databases, queues, and upstream dependencies under time pressure.
Translating product requirements into safe API and data model changes without breaking consumers.
Keeping up with code review, testing, and documentation while sprint commitments stay tight.
Working through unclear ownership when incidents or cross-service bugs span multiple teams.

recommended_question_themes.why: Mid-level backend engineers do best when they have clear ownership of components and enough room to execute independently.
Their effectiveness is shaped by how much time goes to incidents, flaky systems, and support work.
This role regularly makes choices that affect maintainability, compatibility, and downstream teams.
Growth at this level often comes from handling failures well and turning fixes into prevention.
Backend work often stalls on other services, schema changes, or unclear contracts between teams.

terminology.meaning: The agreed request and response shape clients depend on.
A controlled change to database structure or constraints.
Repeating a request has the same effect once.
Returning large result sets in smaller chunks.
A buffer for asynchronous work between systems.
Restricting request volume to protect a service.
Peer feedback on changes before merging.
Automated build, test, and validation steps.
A test that checks components working together.
Reverting a release after problems appear.
Past shortcuts that slow future changes.
Using logs, metrics, and traces to understand systems.
How long a request takes to complete.
How much work a service handles over time.
Allowed unreliability before delivery should slow down.
Being responsible for responding to production issues.
A write-up of what failed and what to improve.

listen_for: Whether they talk about systems in terms of contracts, failure modes, and trade-offs rather than just tickets.
How clearly they separate symptoms from root causes when describing bugs or incidents.
Whether production issues sound like normal ownership or constant firefighting.
Shifts from talking about writing code to talking about operating and improving services.
Whether they mention dependency blockers, unclear ownership, or brittle handoffs with other teams.
avoid: Treating backend work as invisible support for frontend delivery.
Assuming speed matters more than safe migrations, testing, or backward compatibility.
Reading incident involvement as poor performance when it may reflect real service ownership.
Pushing architecture debates far beyond their scope instead of helping with concrete decisions and execution.
